question: how does compost smell
title: How Does Compost Smell? (A Properly Made Pile Shouldn't Stink)
metaDescription: A well-managed hot compost pile smells like fresh earth, not rot. Here's what different compost smells mean, what causes bad odors, and how to fix them.
content:
# How Does Compost Smell? (A Properly Made Pile Shouldn't Stink)

I get asked this question at least once a week, usually from someone curious about composting but a little nervous about what they're getting into. "Scotty, doesn't it smell terrible? You've got a pile of rotting stuff out there. How do your neighbors not hate you?"

A properly made, properly managed compost pile does not smell bad. Not even a little bit. If your compost smells terrible, that's information, it's telling you something is wrong, and the fix is almost always simple.

## What a Good Compost Pile Actually Smells Like

A hot, active compost pile, one that's working the way it's supposed to, smells like fresh earth. Rich, dark, alive. Same smell that hits you after rain on good soil, or when you push your hand into a healthy garden bed. That's petrichor, a compound called geosmin produced by actinobacteria that are part of the decomposition community.

That smell means aerobic decomposition is happening: oxygen-dependent microorganisms are breaking down organic matter, releasing heat, and producing carbon dioxide, water, and that characteristic earthy scent. Cornell's composting resources describe this stage, the thermophilic phase, as the hot, active center of a working pile, with internal temperatures between 40 and 60°C (104 to 140°F), driven by microbial metabolism.

A pile producing that smell is doing everything right. It's alive, it's working, and it's not going to bother your neighbors.

## The Smells That Tell You Something's Wrong

Different bad smells mean different problems. Every one of those problems has a straightforward fix.

**Ammonia smell.** Sharp, nose-burning, like a dirty bathroom. Your pile has too much nitrogen relative to carbon. You've loaded it with grass clippings, food scraps, coffee grounds, or fresh manure without enough "brown" carbonaceous material to balance it out. The excess nitrogen is being volatilized off as ammonia gas.

Fix: add carbon. Wood chips, shredded cardboard, dry leaves, straw. Cornell recommends a carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of approximately 30:1 for optimal decomposition. Most pure food waste is far too nitrogen-heavy. Turn the pile after adding the browns and the smell will clear within a day or two.

**Rotten egg / sulfur smell.** The one that gets people in trouble with neighbors. Sulfurous, foul, distinctly unpleasant. Your pile has gone anaerobic, oxygen-deficient in the core, and anaerobic bacteria have taken over. Anaerobic decomposition produces hydrogen sulfide along with other unpleasant compounds.

The cause is usually too much moisture or too much density, the pile got compacted and waterlogged, and air can't circulate. Cornell's odor management resources note that when anaerobic conditions develop, even the pile's outer aerobic zone can't fully filter and degrade the odorous compounds before they escape.

Fix: turn the pile aggressively to reintroduce oxygen. Add dry, bulky material, wood chips are ideal, to improve structure and drainage. If the pile is genuinely waterlogged, cover it temporarily to stop additional rain and work dry material through the interior.

**Vinegary or sweet-sour smell.** Less offensive than the others, but still an indicator. The pile is getting too acidic, typically from a lot of fruit scraps or sugary materials that ferment quickly. Add more browns, turn to aerate, and the microbial community will re-balance.

## The Moisture Question

More compost odor problems come from moisture imbalance than anything else. Cornell is specific: a pile should have moisture content between 45 and 65 percent, roughly the moisture of a wrung-out sponge. Squeeze a handful. A few drops of water should come out. If it drips freely, it's too wet. If it's dry and dusty, the biology has slowed down.

Too dry: microbial activity slows, the pile doesn't heat up, decomposition stalls.

Too wet: anaerobic conditions develop in the saturated core, sulfur and other odorous byproducts accumulate, the pile slows down and starts to smell.

Fix for wet: turn and add dry, bulky material. Fix for dry: water carefully as you turn, aiming for even moisture distribution throughout.

## What a Working Hot Pile Actually Looks Like

When I'm pitching a pile that's really firing, one that's about a week to ten days old and in full thermophilic activity, you can see steam rising off it when you turn it on a cool morning. That steam is water vapor and CO2 from intense microbial metabolism. Stick a probe in and you'll read between 130 and 160°F. The smell is intense earth, rich, deep, biological.

The microorganisms are consuming organic matter so fast that the biological heat generation exceeds the pile's heat loss. It smells the way healthy soil smells after rain, just more concentrated. It does not smell rotten. It does not smell like garbage.

If you've started a pile and you're not getting steam and heat within a few days, check your carbon-to-nitrogen ratio and your moisture. Those are almost always the two levers that need adjusting.

## Practical Tips for Keeping Things Neighborly

For most home composters, keeping a pile aerobic, properly balanced, and not waterlogged eliminates neighbor concerns entirely. A working aerobic compost pile isn't something most people can smell from ten feet away, let alone a property line.

Site your pile so prevailing winds don't blow toward neighbor's windows. Keep meat, fish, and dairy out of open piles, these are high in protein and fat, decompose differently, and can create real odor problems in a standard compost setup. Use a tumbler or enclosed bin if you're in a dense neighborhood. Turn regularly, weekly is ideal for an active pile. More turning means more oxygen, faster decomposition, less time for any odor to develop.

The EPA confirms that home composting, when done correctly, should not produce significant odors.

Y'all, compost isn't supposed to smell like garbage. If it does, the pile is asking you for something. Learn the language and it becomes a conversation, not a complaint.

---

## Sources

- [Compost Physics, Cornell Composting](https://compost.css.cornell.edu/physics.html)
- [Odor Management, Cornell Composting](https://compost.css.cornell.edu/odors/odor.html)
- [Compost Microorganisms, Cornell Composting](https://compost.css.cornell.edu/microorg.html)
- [Composting At Home, US EPA](https://www.epa.gov/recycle/composting-home)
- [Composting: Balancing Your Greens and Browns, Cornell Waste Management Institute](https://cwmi.css.cornell.edu/balancing.pdf)

scottyQuotes: A properly made compost pile doesn't smell bad. Not even a little bit. If yours stinks, it's asking you for something.
That earthy smell after rain? That's what a working compost pile smells like, more concentrated, just as alive.
Compost isn't supposed to smell like garbage. Learn the language of your pile, and it becomes a conversation, not a complaint.
